title: Large file attachments corrupted during upload via web interface (regression ~1 week ago)
problem: PDF attachments uploaded through the web interface are returned as corrupted/damaged files when downloaded. The corruption is size-dependent: small PDFs appear to upload successfully, while larger files (scanned documents, reports) are consistently broken. The issue began approximately one week ago and affects multiple customers.
root_cause_hypothesis: A recent change (approximately one week ago) to the file upload or storage pipeline is corrupting large files during upload, storage, or retrieval. Likely candidates: a change to chunked upload handling that drops or misordes chunks for large files, a new file size limit or middleware that silently truncates payloads, a change in content encoding or transfer encoding (e.g., base64 vs binary) that mangles large payloads, or a change in file processing/scanning that corrupts files above a certain size threshold.
reproduction_steps: Upload a large PDF (e.g., a multi-page scanned document, likely >5-10MB) to a task via the web interface
Download the same attachment back from the task
Attempt to open the downloaded PDF — it will report as damaged/corrupted
Repeat with a small PDF (e.g., a single-page text-based PDF) — this should succeed
Compare file sizes of the original and downloaded files to identify truncation or inflation
environment: Web interface (browser-based upload). No information on specific browser or OS, but the issue is affecting multiple customers so it is not environment-specific.
impact: Multiple customers who rely on file attachments for their workflows are unable to upload and retrieve large documents. This blocks core task management workflows for teams that attach scanned documents and reports to cases.
recommended_fix: 1. Review all changes to the file upload, storage, and retrieval pipeline from approximately one week ago (check deploy logs, git history, infrastructure changes). 2. Compare the byte content of a large uploaded file in storage against the original to determine where corruption occurs (upload, storage, or download). 3. Check for recent changes to: upload size limits, chunked upload assembly, content-type/encoding handling, file scanning or processing middleware, CDN or proxy configuration. 4. Test whether non-PDF large files are also affected to confirm whether this is file-type-specific or size-specific.
proposed_test_case: Upload files of varying sizes (1MB, 5MB, 10MB, 25MB, 50MB) in multiple formats (PDF, PNG, DOCX) via the web interface, download each, and verify byte-for-byte integrity against the originals using checksums. This identifies the exact size threshold and whether the issue is format-dependent.
information_gaps: Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (developer can determine by testing)
Whether non-PDF large files are also affected (reporter uncertain; developer can verify server-side)
Specific browser(s) used by affected customers (unlikely to be relevant given multi-customer impact)
Exact error message from PDF viewers (reporter described it as 'damaged or corrupted')
